2026-06-27.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysctl (kernel.syscall_user_dispatch) to control whether userspace can arm Syscall User Dispatch (SUD). The default value is 1, allowing arming of SUD. Disarming SUD is always permitted. The sysctl is only present when the kernel is built with CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH. This patch also updates documentation and code to reflect the new sysctl.

reviews.author: Thomas Gleixner
reviews.summary: Raised concerns that an application with SUD enabled prior to the sysctl toggle would suddenly fail in operation if it requires to move the dispatch window. Also suggested improving the change log to explain the reasoning behind the design choice.
reviews.raw_body:
On Sat, Jun 27 2026 at 16:55, Gregory Price wrote:

Same comment vs. subject prefix.

> Add a matching sysctl to go with CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH.
>
> kernel.syscall_user_dispatch (default 1) controls whether userspace
> may arm SUD (both via prctl and ptrace).

What's SUD again? This is a changelog and not twatter.

> +	/* Arming can be denied at runtime via sysctl, disarming is allowed */
> +	if (mode != PR_SYS_DISPATCH_OFF && !syscall_user_dispatch_allowed)
> +		return -EPERM;

That might be similar to other sysctls, but if an application had it
enabled prior to the sysctl=off toggle, then that application will
suddenly fail in operation if it requires to move the dispatch window.

Thanks,

        tglx

reviews.author: Thomas Gleixner
reviews.summary: Reiterated the need for a clear explanation of the design choice in the change log, and suggested that a hard disable would result in undefined behavior.
reviews.raw_body:
On Fri, Jul 03 2026 at 13:26, Gregory Price wrote:
>> > +	/* Arming can be denied at runtime via sysctl, disarming is allowed */
>> > +	if (mode != PR_SYS_DISPATCH_OFF && !syscall_user_dispatch_allowed)
>> > +		return -EPERM;
>> 
>> That might be similar to other sysctls, but if an application had it
>> enabled prior to the sysctl=off toggle, then that application will
>> suddenly fail in operation if it requires to move the dispatch window.
>>
>
> If the admin is turning it off globally, is that not exactly what the
> admin wants?  The alternative is a hard disable that will simply result
> in undefined behavior (windows syscall ABI being interpreted at a linux
> syscall) instead of failing gracefully on the re-arm.
>
> This seemed like the better option.

Fair enough, but please explain such reasoning properly in the change
log.

reviews.author: Gregory Price
reviews.summary: Responded to Thomas Gleixner's concerns by explaining that a soft disable would allow the application to fail gracefully, whereas a hard disable would result in undefined behavior.
reviews.raw_body:
> > +	/* Arming can be denied at runtime via sysctl, disarming is allowed */
> > +	if (mode != PR_SYS_DISPATCH_OFF && !syscall_user_dispatch_allowed)
> > +		return -EPERM;
> 
> That might be similar to other sysctls, but if an application had it
> enabled prior to the sysctl=off toggle, then that application will
> suddenly fail in operation if it requires to move the dispatch window.
>

If the admin is turning it off globally, is that not exactly what the
admin wants?  The alternative is a hard disable that will simply result
in undefined behavior (windows syscall ABI being interpreted at a linux
syscall) instead of failing gracefully on the re-arm.

This seemed like the better option.

~Gregory

2026-07-03.patch_summary: This patch series adds a sysctl to control whether userspace can arm Syscall User Dispatch (SUD) via prctl or ptrace. The sysctl, kernel.syscall_user_dispatch, defaults to permitting SUD and is only present when the kernel is built with CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH. The change log provides documentation for this new sysctl.
